content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886


February 3, 2010


Fred Keeley
Trustee, California Ocean Science Trust
Treasurer, County of Santa Cruz
P.O. Box 1817
Santa Cruz, California 95061

Re:    Your Request for Advice
       Our File No. A-09-202

Dear Mr. Keeley:

     This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of members of The
Ocean Science Trust ("OST") regarding the conflict-of-interest code provisions of the
Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

QUESTION

     Is OST a state agency for purposes of the Political Reform Act, and thus required
to create and maintain a conflict-of-interest code?

CONCLUSION

     Though OST may not be considered a state agency in all contexts under the law,
the OST is considered a state agency for purposes of the Political Reform Act and,
therefore, must develop and maintain a conflict-of-interest code because OST's impetus
for formation originated with a government agency, it operates with the use of a
substantial amount of state funding, performs services that public agencies are legally
authorized to perform and which, in fact they have traditionally performed and is treated
as a public entity by certain statutory provisions.


¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All
statutory references are to Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the
Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the
California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of
Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-09-202
Page No. 2

FACTS

The Ocean Science Trust is a 501(c)(3) nonprofit corporation subject to federal
and California nonprofit laws. In 2000, the California Ocean Resources Stewardship Act
of 2000 was passed by the Legislature. The bill authorized the Secretary of the
Resources Agency to enter into an agreement with an existing nonprofit corporation to
establish a trust to be known as the California Ocean Trust to seek and provide funding
for ocean resource science projects and to encourage coordinated, multiagency,
multimstitution approaches to ocean resource science. On September 12, 2003, Mary D.
Nichols, the Secretary of the California Resources Agency filed the Articles of
incorporation that created OST. Secretary Nichols signed the articles of incorporation as
the Initial Director for OST. She was also the agent for service of process and listed the
address of the California Resources Agency as the contact address for the agent for
service of process.

OST receives funding from a variety of sources. In 2007, OST received
approximately 18 percent of its funding from private sources, while the remaining 82
percent came from federal funds through the Coastal Impact Assistance Program, which
were administered by the California Natural Resources Agency. In 2008, OST received
about 33 percent from the state, 25 percent from the federal government administered by
the California Natural Resources Agency, and 42 percent of its funding from private
sources. For 2009 OST projects that it will obtain 66 percent of its funding from the
state, 9 percent from the federal government and 25 percent from private sources. In the
future, OST hopes to increase the level of private funding. OST receives all of its
funding on a project by project basis and it submits a detailed proposal for each project
for which it seeks funding. OST does not receive a set amount of operational funding
from a state or other government agency each year.

OST's mission is to serve as a credible, unbiased source of science for
policymakers and others concerned with ocean conservation. OST was officially set up
as a nongovernmental trust. OST works to operate independent of state interests and
politics, so that it can be viewed by all stakeholders (e.g., environmental groups,
commercial and industrial entities, and politicians) as an independent resource for sound
ocean science and policy.

OST has 10 trustees all of whom are appointed by the Secretary of the California
Resources Agency, the California Secretary for Environmental Protection, or the
California Director of Finance. Additionally, to the extent feasible, the trustees appointed
to the trust are to balance and reflect the breadth of public interests concerned with ocean
resources. (See Public Resources Code Section 36992(d).) Finally, according to OST's
website, OST provides science services to California state agencies and coordinating
bodies such as the Ocean Protection Council, serves the state by coordinating expert
advice, and acts as liaison and bridging institution, and assists in improving coordination
among scientists, policymakers and resource managers, focusing specifically on issues
that are a priority for the latter.

File No. A-09-202
Page No. 3

OST is not subject to organizational audits by the state but is subject to state audit only for specific state funded projects under the terms of the contract for that particular project.

ANALYSIS

Section 87300 of the Act requires every agency to adopt and promulgate a conflict-of-interest code. In a formal opinion, the Commission developed an analytical framework to determine whether seemingly private entities are public agencies under the Act. (In re Siegel (1977) 3 FPPC Ops.) In the Siegel opinion, the Commission held that a nonprofit corporation formed for the purpose of acquiring and operating a water system was a public agency under the Act. On its face, the nonprofit corporation appeared to be a private entity. To determine the true nature of the entity for purposes of the Act, the Commission developed four criteria. The four criteria are:

1. Did the impetus for formation of the entity originate with a government entity?

2. Is the entity substantially funded by, or is its primary source of funds, a government agency?

3. Is one of the principal purposes for which the entity is formed to provide services or undertake obligations that public agencies are legally authorized to perform and which in fact, they traditionally have performed?

4. Is the entity treated as a public entity by other statutory provisions?

Starting with the first Siegel criterion, the impetus for formation of OST began with the California Legislature, a government agency. Generally, the first criterion is met where an entity is created by statute or ordinance or by some official action of another government agency. (Moas Advice Letter, No. A-98-261; Moser Advice Letter, No. A-97-400a.) OST's enabling statute, the California Ocean Resource Stewardship Act of 2000 ("CORSA") authorized the Secretary of the Resources Agency to partner with an existing nonprofit to create OST. In OST's "Report to the Legislature" for March 2007 through September 31, 2008, OST also described itself as having been established by the California Secretary for Resources pursuant to CORSA. Additionally, it was the Secretary of the Resources Agency who served as the "Initial Director" and initial agent for service of process for OST. The Secretary also filed the articles of incorporation for OST. Therefore, because the impetus for formation of OST began with the Legislature and subsequently the California Secretary of Resources, the first Siegel criterion has been met.

File No. A-09-202
Page No. 4

The second Siegel criterion addresses receipt of government funding. OST
receives a substantial amount of funding from the government. While the amount of
funding varies from year to year, for the past three years OST has received over half of its
annual funding from either the state or federal government. Therefore, the second Siegel
criterion has been met because OST receives a substantial amount of funding from
government agencies.

Looking to the third of the Siegel criteria, it is clear that OST performs functions
that public agencies are authorized to perform and in fact traditionally have performed.
Long standing public policy, acknowledged by the United States Supreme Court in 1892,
establishes that a state's title to its tide and submerged lands is different from the title to
the lands it holds for sale. "It is title that is held in trust for the people of the State that
they may enjoy the navigation of the waters, carry on commerce over them . . . free from
obstruction or interference from private parties." The ocean is a natural resource that
belongs to the people and any regulation of it or manipulation of it is done on behalf of
the public. (See Illinois Central R.R. Co. v. Illinois (1892) 146 U.S. 387, 452.) "So with
trusts connected with public property, or property of a special character, like lands under
navigable waters, they cannot be placed entirely beyond the direction and control of the
State." (Id. at 454.)

Further, Siegel looks at whether the entity in question serves a public function and
at the public character of the entity in question. The opinion considers the extent to
which a nonprofit corporation is acting on behalf of a political subdivision of the state.
The California State Legislature has stated that it is the policy of the state to do the
following:

"1. Ensure adequate coordination of ocean resources management science
among state, regional, and federal agencies and marine science
institutions.

"2. Ensure the most efficient and effective use of state resources devoted
to ocean resource management science and encourage the contribution of
federal and non-governmental resources.

"3. Advance applied ocean science, graduate level education and
technology development to meet current and future California ocean
resource management needs."
(Pub. Resources Code, Section 36972.)

The principal purposes for which OST were formed include:

"1. To seek funds for California ocean resource science projects,
emphasizing the development of new funding sources.

File No. A-09-202
Page No. 5

"2. To find California ocean resource science projects that help fulfill the
missions of the state's ocean resource management agencies.

"3. To encourage coordinated, multiagency, multiinstitution approaches to
ocean resource science.

"4. To encourage graduate education programs in management-oriented
ocean resources science in public and private universities and colleges in
California.

"5. To encourage new technologies that reduce the cost, increase the
amount or improve the quality of ocean resource management
information.

"6. To promote more effective coordination of California ocean resource
science useful to management agencies."
(Pub. Resources Code Section 36990.)

When comparing the Legislature's state policy above to OST's purposes it
appears that OST's purposes are very closely aligned to the state policies established by
the Legislature. Therefore, the purpose of OST is to serve a public function.

Siegel also found that government officials having a right to disapprove the name
of anyone submitted to serve on the board of a nonprofit is evidence of the public
function of the nonprofit. (In re Siegel, supra.) OST has 10 trustees all of whom are
appointed, respectively, by the Secretary of the California Resources Agency, the
California Secretary for Environmental Protection, or the California Director of Finance.
Additionally, to the extent feasible, the trustees appointed to the trust are to balance and
reflect the breadth of public interests concerned with ocean resources. (See Public
Resources Code Section 36992(d).) Finally, according to OST's website, OST provides
science services to California state agencies and coordinating bodies such as the Ocean
Protection Council, serves the state by coordinating expert advice, and acts as liaison and
bridging institutions, and assists in improving coordination among scientists, policymakers
and resource managers, focusing specifically on issues that are a priority for the latter.
Based upon this, OST provides services that public agencies are legally authorized to
perform and have traditionally performed. Therefore, the third Siegel criterion has been
met.

The fourth criterion under the Siegel analysis is whether or not the entity in
question is treated as a public entity by other laws. In this case, OST is treated as a
public entity by other laws. CORSA, OST's enabling statute, sets out criteria for how
OST's board members are to be selected and the functions that OST is authorized to
perform. OST is also subject to audit by the Bureau of State Audits at least for the
projects for which it receives state funding. (Pub. Resources Code 36994(a)(5).) The
Bureau of State Audits is the State's independent external auditor. (Pub. Resources Code

File No. A-09-202
Page No. 6

Section 36994.) All audits performed by the State Auditor must follow the Government
Auditing Standards published by the Comptroller General of the United States. The State
Auditor's audit standards requirements are found in Government Code Sections 8546,
8546.1, and 8546.4. Therefore, the fourth Siegel criterion has also been satisfied.

It is not necessary that all four of the Siegel criteria be satisfied for an entity to be
considered a local government agency. (In re Vonk (1981) 6 FPPC Ops. 1; O'Shea
Advice Letter, No. A-91-570.) It is only necessary that the entity satisfy enough of the
four criteria for its overall character to correspond to that of a local government agency.
(Rasiah Advice Letter, No. A-01-020.) Though OST operates as a nonprofit entity, the
true character of OST is public in nature. All four criterion of the Siegel analysis have
been satisfied and OST should be treated as if it were a governmental agency for
purposes of the Act, thus OST is required to maintain a conflict-of-interest code.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5560.

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel

By: Sukhi K. Brar
Counsel, Legal Division

SK:jgl

cc:    Stacy Kray, Counsel, Ocean Science Trust
       Skadden, Arps, Slate, Meagher & Flom LLP
